metadata.author: douglasswm
metadata.notes: Tenancy deposit dispute: alleged paint damage vs. fair-wear-and-tear. Tests landlord/tenant common-law principles + claim quantification under SCT $20,000 limit.

raw_documents.content: I rented Unit #08-12, 45 Tampines Street 21 from 1 January 2024 to 31 December 2025. The agreed deposit was $2,400 (one month's rent). I returned the keys on 30 December 2025 with a clean handover. On 10 February 2026 the landlord deducted the entire deposit citing 'wall damage and excessive cleaning'. The wall scuffs were normal wear after 24 months of tenancy. I have move-in photos dated 1 Jan 2024 showing the same scuffs already present. I demand return of the full $2,400.

Skyview Properties' deduction of $2,400 covers: full unit repaint $1,800, professional deep clean $400, replacement of damaged blind $200. Tenant smoked indoors despite the no-smoking lease clause (s.7.3) — yellowing on ceiling and walls is documented in our 1 Jan 2026 inspection report. Photos enclosed.

Tenancy Agreement dated 28 December 2023. Term: 24 months from 1 Jan 2024. Monthly rent $2,400. Security deposit $2,400 refundable within 14 days of handover, less reasonable deductions for damage beyond fair wear and tear. Clause 7.3: no smoking inside the unit. Clause 9: tenant returns unit in clean condition.

legal_rules: Civil Law Act 1909 s.6 (good faith in tenancy)
Small Claims Tribunals Act 1984 s.5 (jurisdictional limit $20,000)
